skill_name: Verb Complementation: Advanced (Verb + to-inf / -ing with Meaning Change)

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I clearly remember ___ (lock) the front door before we left for the airport, so it must be secure.
distractor_rationale: remember + to-infinitive means a duty to be done in the future, but the sentence describes a past action the speaker recalls doing
bare past participle cannot follow remember as a complement in this construction
bare infinitive without to cannot follow remember as a complement

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The children finally stopped ___ (argue) when their father came into the room and asked them to be quiet.
distractor_rationale: stop + to-infinitive means pausing in order to do something else, but the sentence means they ceased arguing entirely
bare past form cannot follow stop as a gerund complement
bare infinitive without to cannot follow stop as a gerund complement

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Please remember ___ (send) the report to your manager before five o'clock this afternoon.
distractor_rationale: remember + -ing describes recalling a past action already done, but the sentence is a reminder about a future duty
bare past participle cannot follow remember as an infinitive complement
bare infinitive without to cannot follow remember as a complement in standard grammar

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Mark was walking home when he stopped ___ (check) the map on his phone because he wasn't sure of the route.
distractor_rationale: stop + -ing means ceasing an activity, but the sentence means he paused his walking in order to check the map
bare past form cannot follow stop as an infinitive complement
bare infinitive without to cannot follow stop as an infinitive complement in standard grammar

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna forgot ___ (turn off) the oven after dinner, and when she came home later, it was still on.
distractor_rationale: forget + -ing means failing to remember a past experience, but the sentence describes a duty she failed to carry out
bare past participle cannot follow forget as an infinitive complement
bare infinitive without to cannot follow forget as a complement in standard grammar

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The soup is too salty, so why don't you try ___ (add) a little sugar to balance the flavour?
distractor_rationale: try + to-infinitive means making an effort to achieve something difficult, but the sentence suggests experimenting with a possible solution
bare past form cannot follow try as a gerund complement
bare infinitive without to cannot follow try as a complement in standard grammar

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I will never forget ___ (see) the northern lights for the first time — it was the most beautiful thing I had ever witnessed.
distractor_rationale: forget + to-infinitive means failing to carry out a duty, but the sentence describes an unforgettable past experience
bare past form cannot follow forget as a gerund complement
bare infinitive without to cannot follow forget as a complement in standard grammar

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The doctor tried ___ (explain) the diagnosis clearly, but the patient was too anxious to take in all the information at once.
distractor_rationale: try + -ing means experimenting with a method to see if it works, but the sentence describes an effortful attempt at a difficult task
bare past form cannot follow try as an infinitive complement
bare infinitive without to cannot follow try as a complement in standard grammar

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: After working at the company for ten years, Sara went on ___ (become) the head of the entire marketing department.
distractor_rationale: go on + -ing means continuing the same activity without stopping, but the sentence describes a subsequent achievement that came after her earlier work
bare past form cannot follow go on as an infinitive complement
bare infinitive without to cannot follow go on as a complement in standard grammar

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Even though the meeting had already run over time, the manager went on ___ (discuss) every single point on the agenda without any break.
distractor_rationale: go on + to-infinitive means moving on to a new activity after finishing the previous one, but the sentence makes clear the manager continued the same activity without stopping
bare past form cannot follow go on as a gerund complement
bare infinitive without to cannot follow go on as a complement in standard grammar

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I clearly remember ___ (lock) the front door before we left for the airport, so I don't know why you think we forgot.
distractor_rationale: remember + to-infinitive means a duty to be performed in the future, not a memory of a completed past action
a bare past participle cannot follow remember as a complement; the gerund is required for past memory
a bare infinitive without 'to' cannot follow remember in standard grammar

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The doctor told Mark to stop ___ (eat) fast food if he wanted to improve his health.
distractor_rationale: stop + to-infinitive means to pause in order to do something new, not to cease an activity; the context clearly means ceasing the habit
a bare infinitive without 'to' cannot follow stop as a complement in this structure
a past participle cannot follow stop as a complement; the gerund is required for the meaning of ceasing

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna forgot ___ (send) the report to her manager before the deadline, so her manager never received it.
distractor_rationale: forget + -ing describes a past event that has been forgotten from memory; the context shows the report was never sent, meaning a duty was neglected, which requires forget + to-infinitive
a bare infinitive without 'to' cannot follow forget as a complement in this structure
forget + having + past participle is not a standard construction and does not match the meaning of a neglected duty

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The soup was too salty, so Lisa tried ___ (add) a little sugar to balance the flavour, and it actually worked.
distractor_rationale: try + to-infinitive means to make an attempt at something difficult; the context shows Lisa is experimenting with a remedy she has not tried before, which requires try + -ing
a past form cannot follow try as a complement in this structure
a bare infinitive without 'to' cannot follow try as a complement in standard grammar

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: He was halfway through his speech when he stopped ___ (check) his notes, then continued speaking with more confidence.
distractor_rationale: stop + -ing means to cease an activity permanently or habitually; the context shows he paused his speech in order to perform the action of checking notes, which requires stop + to-infinitive to express purpose
a bare infinitive without 'to' cannot follow stop as a complement in this structure
stop + having + past participle is not a standard construction and does not correctly express the meaning of pausing in order to do something

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Please stop ___ so loudly — the baby is trying to sleep in the next room.
distractor_rationale: stop + to-infinitive means pausing in order to do something, not ceasing an action
'to talking' is not a grammatical construction in English
bare infinitive after stop is not grammatical in this complementation pattern

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Do you remember ___ your keys on the kitchen table before you left for work yesterday?
distractor_rationale: remember + to-infinitive means recalling a duty to perform, not recalling a past event that already happened
'to leaving' is not a grammatical construction in English
past tense form cannot follow remember as a complement in this structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: She stopped ___ a coffee on her way to the office because she was already late.
distractor_rationale: stop + -ing means ceasing an action, but here she paused her journey in order to buy coffee, so -ing gives the wrong meaning
'to buying' is not a grammatical construction in English
bare infinitive after stop is not grammatical in this complementation pattern

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: I forgot ___ the electricity bill last month, so now I have to pay a late fee as well.
distractor_rationale: forget + -ing describes failing to remember a past experience, not a duty that was left undone; here the bill was not paid because she forgot the obligation
'to paying' is not a grammatical construction in English
past tense form cannot follow forget as a complement in this structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The heating system in the flat kept breaking down, so the engineer tried ___ a different setting on the thermostat to see if it helped.
distractor_rationale: try + to-infinitive means making an effort or attempt, often implying difficulty; try + -ing means experimenting with a method to see if it works, which fits the context of testing a different setting
'to adjusting' is not a grammatical construction in English
past tense form cannot follow try as a complement in this structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Remember ___ the forms before the deadline on Friday, or your application will not be accepted.
distractor_rationale: remember + -ing refers to recalling a past event that already happened; here the action is a future duty that must not be forgotten
'to submitting' is not a grammatical construction in English
past tense form cannot follow remember as a complement in this structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: I will never forget ___ my grandmother's house for the first time as a child — the smell of fresh bread was amazing.
distractor_rationale: forget + to-infinitive describes failing to remember a duty; here the speaker is saying they will always remember a past experience, so -ing is required
'to visiting' is not a grammatical construction in English
past tense form cannot follow forget as a complement in this structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The manager went on ___ the same problems with the team's communication even after everyone had agreed to make changes.
distractor_rationale: go on + to-infinitive means moving on to a new, different action after finishing the current one; go on + -ing means continuing the same action, which fits the context of repeatedly mentioning the same problems
'to describing' is not a grammatical construction in English
past tense form cannot follow go on as a complement in this structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: After finishing his opening remarks, the speaker went on ___ the main results of the research project to the audience.
distractor_rationale: go on + -ing means continuing the same action without stopping; go on + to-infinitive means moving on to a new, different action, which fits the context of finishing one part and beginning another
'to presenting' is not a grammatical construction in English
past tense form cannot follow go on as a complement in this structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: We regret ___ you that your application has been unsuccessful on this occasion, but we encourage you to apply again next year.
distractor_rationale: regret + -ing describes feeling sorry about a past action one has already done; regret + to-infinitive is a formal expression used when delivering bad news at the moment of speaking, which fits this context
'to informing' is not a grammatical construction in English
past tense form cannot follow regret as a complement in this structure

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She stopped to talk so loudly when she noticed everyone was looking at her.
content.error_sentence: She stopped to talk so loudly when she noticed everyone was looking at her.
content.corrected_sentence: She stopped talking so loudly when she noticed everyone was looking at her.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I remember to visit my grandmother every Sunday when I was a child.
content.error_sentence: I remember to visit my grandmother every Sunday when I was a child.
content.corrected_sentence: I remember visiting my grandmother every Sunday when I was a child.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: He forgot posting the letter, so the bill arrived late and he had to pay a fine.
content.error_sentence: He forgot posting the letter, so the bill arrived late and he had to pay a fine.
content.corrected_sentence: He forgot to post the letter, so the bill arrived late and he had to pay a fine.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: When the car broke down on the motorway, Tom stopped checking the map and called a mechanic instead.
content.error_sentence: When the car broke down on the motorway, Tom stopped checking the map and called a mechanic instead.
content.corrected_sentence: When the car broke down on the motorway, Tom stopped to check the map and called a mechanic instead.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If the heating isn't working, try to turn the thermostat off and on again to see if that fixes the problem.
content.error_sentence: If the heating isn't working, try to turn the thermostat off and on again to see if that fixes the problem.
content.corrected_sentence: If the heating isn't working, try turning the thermostat off and on again to see if that fixes the problem.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: After finishing the first report, she went on to explain the new project for another hour, which exhausted everyone in the meeting.
content.error_sentence: After finishing the first report, she went on to explain the new project for another hour, which exhausted everyone in the meeting.
content.corrected_sentence: After finishing the first report, she went on explaining the new project for another hour, which exhausted everyone in the meeting.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The manager regrets to inform the team that the annual bonus has already been paid and cannot be changed.
content.error_sentence: The manager regrets to inform the team that the annual bonus has already been paid and cannot be changed.
content.corrected_sentence: The manager regrets informing the team that the annual bonus has already been paid and cannot be changed.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Taking this job means to give up the flexible schedule you currently enjoy, so think carefully before you decide.
